original_abstract:
High-resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT), or micro-CT, has emerged as a premier non-destructive imaging tool in the geosciences, enabling the three-dimensional visualization of internal structures in geological materials. This review evaluates the fundamental principles, technological advancements, and practical limitations of micro-CT as it transitions into a standard characterization technique. Significant advantages include the ability to perform 4D monitoring—tracking structural dynamics over time—and achieving spatial resolutions reaching the sub-micron scale. However, the technique faces challenges such as imaging artifacts (e.g., beam hardening and cone-beam effects), discretization errors, and the inherent trade-off between sample size and achievable resolution. Furthermore, the accuracy of quantitative 3D analysis often remains sensitive to operator-dependent variables during data reconstruction and segmentation.

The paper provides an extensive overview of current applications across diverse geoscience disciplines, including 3D pore network characterization, grain morphology analysis, fracture modeling, ore mineralogy, and the study of paleontological specimens. It also explores emerging trends such as multi-scale data fusion, where micro-CT is integrated with complementary techniques like SEM or PET to correlate structural and chemical information. As computational power and X-ray optics continue to evolve, micro-CT is positioned to move beyond qualitative imaging to become an essential, high-precision quantitative tool for understanding the complex physical and chemical processes within Earth materials.
humanized_abstract:
High-Resolution X-ray Computed Tomography in the Geosciences: Fundamentals, Applications, and Future Developments High-Resolution X-ray Computed Tomography (HRXCT) or micro-CT is an essential non-destructive imaging method in the geosciences used for 3D imaging of the geometry of the geological materials. In this review, the authors attempt to evaluate the basics, developments and application of micro-CT as it has evolved into an effective and standard characterization tool. The major advantage of micro-CT lies in 4D monitoring of rock behaviour and also achieving a spatial resolution of sub-micron scale. It also has certain disadvantages including effects of artifacts like beam hardening and cone-beam effect, discretization errors and the effect of trade-off between sample size and resolution of micro-CT images. The authors also discuss the accuracy of 3D quantitative analysis and its sensitivity towards the variables which depend on the operator during data processing and volume segmentation.

The paper offers a detailed review of the present applications of X-ray micro-CT in geosciences. Examples ranging from 3D pore network analysis, grain morphology analysis, fracture modeling, ore mineralogy to paleontology are given in detail. Emerging trends like multi-scale data fusion, combining X-ray micro-CT with additional techniques (e.g. SEM or PET-CT) to obtain complementary structural and/or compositional information are highlighted. Thanks to the development of computing power and X-ray optics, X-ray micro-CT will probably move towards quantitative applications based on high precision, allowing the understanding of the physical and chemical processes acting in the Earth materials.
